free_text: Fusion suppression in mass-asymetric reactions leading
to Ra compound nuclei.
The targets were metal evaporations of highly enriched
isotopes Pb-204,206,208 and Er-168,170, thicknesses
150-200-mug/cm**2, onto thin carbon backings,
thicknesses 15-20-mug/cm**2.
The beam intensity was monitored continuously
using four silicon surface-barrier detector to measure
Rutherford scattering from the target.
evaporation residues were detected.
Fission fragments were detected by the two-arm
time-of-flight(TOF) spectrometer CORSET. Each arm of
the spectrometer consisted of a compact start detector
and a stop position-sensitive detector
For the Ca-48 reactions, the separated evaporation
residues passed through the start detector of the TOF
system and were implanted into a silicon surface-
barrier stop detector(SSBD). The resolution of this
system allowed to distinguish ERs unambiguously in the
E-TOF spectra. For the C-12 reactions, the energy
resolution(less or equal 30 keV FWHM) of the SSBD for
5-10 MeV alpha particles allowed to identify various
Ra-216,218,220 evaporation channels from their known
alpha-energies and those of their daughter products.
The XTU-Tandem  of the Laboratori
Nazionali di Legnaro.
Evaporation residues recoiling from the
target were separated from the intense flux of beamlike
particles using an electrostatic deflector(ED).
The ER cross sections obtained at 3-degree and
1-degree were integrated using a Gaussian to the
measured angular distributions for Ra-214 produced in
the C-12+Pb-206 reaction at E(lab)=73 MeV and for ERs
detected in the Ca-48+Er-170 reaction at E(lab)=204
MeV. Angular distributions were measured by rotating
the ED around the target position.
The integrated cross sections were corrected for the
ED transmission efficiency using a Monte Carlo
simulation developed for this purpose.
